Q: Used GMAT Club for Quant practice, working topic by topic from 605-655 level questions before increasing difficulty.
Prioritized arithmetic and algebra first, then moved into functions and permutations/combinations.
Kept an error log and used sectionals and official mocks to identify the question types most likely to appear.
Treated most Quant questions as solvable in a few steps and looked for efficient routes rather than heavy calculation.
Re-read the question after solving to avoid marking a trap answer or misreading the prompt.
For timing issues, isolated which Quant question types consumed too much time and improved those specifically.
V: Used the Official Guide for Verbal practice.
Relied on elimination as the main technique for both RC and CR.
For assumption CR, looked for the statement without which the argument could not stand.
Started with lower-difficulty Verbal questions and spent more time thinking through the reasoning instead of simply doing high volume.
DI: Prepared DI alongside the other sections rather than leaving it until the end.
When MSR or another DI set was not yielding progress, avoided spending too much time forcing it.
overall: Used official mocks regularly instead of waiting until the end of prep.
Combined GMAT Club practice with an error log and targeted review.
Treated mock accuracy as only one signal and used full mocks to judge timing and score readiness.
In the final week, kept practice light with daily warm-up questions and avoided overworking.

Q: Moved from free, unstructured study to a guided program that rebuilt Quant fundamentals and provided a large topic-by-topic question bank.
Used repeated practice to become comfortable with common Quant patterns rather than relying only on school math ability.
overall: After underestimating the test on the first attempt, adopted a structured study schedule for the retake.
Used roughly six weeks of active preparation before the second official attempt.
Benefited from having more time and lower stress during the retake cycle.

Q: Started from a strong Quant base and made Quant the section to maximize for the overall score.
Built confidence with medium questions, then emphasized hard questions to prepare for a Q85+ level test.
Expected several hard Quant questions on test day and practiced 700+ material so that difficulty felt familiar.
Started the exam with Quant, finished early, and used the remaining time to review carefully.
DI: Identified timing, not concepts, as the main DI problem.
Stopped spending five or six minutes on a few difficult DI questions when that would force rushed guesses later.
Adopted strategic skipping: protect time for solvable questions instead of trying to get every DI item right.
Framed DI as a section where choosing battles mattered more than perfection.
overall: Took an official practice test cold to establish a baseline and target range.
Built a two-month plan around efficiency because prep had to fit around long work hours.
Focused on one section at a time rather than switching randomly between topics.
Tested multiple section orders in practice and chose to begin with the strongest section for confidence.
Used e-GMAT for the two-month prep window and took two official mocks.

Q: Completed large numbers of medium and hard practice questions to build familiarity with Quant question types.
Developed solving habits through repeated quizzes, combining course methods with personal shortcuts that made problems easier to execute.
After mock review exposed recurring weak topics, retook targeted quizzes on those topics before the second official attempt.
V: Used repeated practice to become more familiar with Verbal question types and improve accuracy.
DI: Used high-volume practice to build familiarity with DI question types.
Noted that the official DI section felt harder partly because strong performance in other sections may have raised difficulty.
Recognized Data Sufficiency as appearing heavily on the official test and treated it as a pattern to prepare for.
overall: Prepared for seven months, relying mostly on TTP and retaking official mocks 1-3.
Used mocks to become comfortable with timing, format, and staying focused into the third section.
After plateauing at 645, treated the first 675 mock as proof the target was reachable.
Between the first and second official attempts, reviewed mock errors, logged recurring topics, and shifted to targeted practice.

Q: Used TTP mainly for volume, repetition, and consistency rather than for new math content.
Identified weaker areas such as real-world word problems and permutations/combinations.
Chose to skip time-consuming P&C questions quickly instead of fighting them at the expense of the section.
Learned to distinguish between a hard question worth investing in and an ego-driven time trap.
Tracked timing checkpoints during practice so that finishing the section became non-negotiable.
V: Stopped assuming Verbal was fine just because CR was strong.
Diagnosed RC inference as the real weakness and addressed it directly.
Slowed down on the first read of RC passages instead of rushing to questions after a superficial understanding.
Wrote short rough-sheet notes while reading and mentally structured each passage before answering questions.
Used deliberate practice and patience rather than adding complicated Verbal techniques.
DI: Treated the low DI score as a consequence of neglecting the section during prep.
Focused heavily on Data Sufficiency after it emerged as the main DI bottleneck.
Worked through the official 100-question DI bank twice so that question types became familiar.
Used repetition to reduce overthinking and panic on DI.
Concluded that DI should be prepared in parallel with Quant from the start.
overall: After several attempts, shifted from learning more content to improving execution under time pressure.
Made finishing every section and avoiding blanks non-negotiable.
Practiced timed from the start and treated untimed practice as a source of bad habits.
Used repeated exposure through a mistake tracker rather than a complex error-log system.
Kept the exam in perspective and focused on calm execution instead of tying identity to the score.

Q: Analyzed mocks to identify specific weak Quant topics, then revised and practiced those areas.
Focused on being able to solve whatever appeared rather than chasing only very hard questions.
Used the Official Guide, GMAT Club, and e-GMAT for practice.
V: Moved Verbal to the first section because doing it last caused fatigue and weaker focus.
Watched for missed keywords and avoidable errors under time pressure.
DI: Placed DI after Quant in the final section order.
Expected DI on the real test to feel slightly tougher than official mocks.
overall: Moved from a 635-645 mock plateau to a 695 official score by improving focus and execution rather than adding many new concepts.
Reviewed mocks carefully to identify patterns in mistakes and weak areas.
Used a section order of Verbal, Quant, then DI, with the break after Verbal to reset.
Studied around work with more time on weekends, shorter weekday sessions, and leave before the exam for final focus.
Added small daily practice by having friends send a handful of questions to solve before sleep.
